heuristics.rule: Start internship search 9–12 months before start date; start full-time search 6–12 months before graduation; for international students on OPT, start 12–18 months before graduation
heuristics.condition: When planning your job search timeline...
heuristics.action: ...anchor your start date to your target end date and work backward, treating job search as a concurrent multi-semester project rather than a post-graduation task
heuristics.because: By the time urgency is felt, the best options (early referrals, relationship-building cycles, insider knowledge) have already been foreclosed

heuristics.rule: Allocate 50%+ of job search time to cold outreach and referral requests, not to applications or resume polishing
heuristics.condition: When deciding how to spend your daily job search hours...
heuristics.action: ...prioritize Type A activities (cold outreach, referral requests, networking with strangers) over Type B (applying to postings) and Type C (browsing, optimizing resume abstractly)
heuristics.because: Type A has the highest return per hour invested even though response rates per message are low; Type C feels productive but generates zero pipeline

heuristics.rule: The further down the referral chain you are from a close personal contact, the more proof of worth your outreach message must contain
heuristics.condition: When reaching out to someone at a target company with no strong existing relationship...
heuristics.action: ...front-load your outreach with concrete evidence of capability (specific outcomes, relevant work, common ground) rather than a generic introduction
heuristics.because: A cold contact with no obvious connection has no social obligation to forward your materials; demonstrated value substitutes for relational trust

heuristics.rule: Never ask for a job or referral in the first informational interview conversation
heuristics.condition: When conducting a 20–30 minute informational interview with someone at a target company...
heuristics.action: ...ask only for knowledge (their path, team challenges, what strong performance looks like) and demonstrate genuine curiosity about their experience
heuristics.because: Social obligation to move your resume forward is created by genuine connection, not transactional requests; premature asks destroy the relationship before it forms

heuristics.rule: Apply to companies you have researched, not companies you have merely heard of
heuristics.condition: When building your target company list...
heuristics.action: ...require yourself to have specific knowledge of each company's products, tech stack, culture, or recent news before adding it to your active pipeline
heuristics.because: Generic interest is detectable by hiring managers and fails the 'specific reason why this company' test that distinguishes hired candidates from rejected ones

heuristics.rule: Optimize your LinkedIn for recruiter search behavior (headlines, skills, keywords) rather than for narrative readability
heuristics.action: ...prioritize keyword density and searchability in headlines and skills sections to drive inbound recruiter discovery, then use the narrative sections to drive conversion
heuristics.because: LinkedIn functions as a search engine; recruiters searching for candidates will never reach your narrative if your profile doesn't surface in their query results

heuristics.rule: Practice behavioral interview responses out loud with another person, not silently in your head
heuristics.action: ...drill STAR responses verbally with a live partner rather than reading about the framework or rehearsing internally
heuristics.because: Answering out loud under social pressure is a distinct skill from understanding a framework; internal rehearsal does not build the muscle being tested

heuristics.rule: Track funding announcements and hiring announcements at target companies to identify unlisted roles
heuristics.condition: When trying to access the hidden job market...
heuristics.action: ...monitor companies in active growth phases because they frequently fill new headcount before roles are posted publicly
heuristics.because: Companies adding headcount under growth pressure prioritize speed; roles get filled through internal networks before HR completes the formal posting process

decision_factors.why_it_matters: A close friend who can vouch for you personally creates a fundamentally different social obligation than a cold contact with no common ground; the conversion rate and required effort differ dramatically across the five levels
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map each target company contact against the five-tier referral hierarchy: (1) close friend who vouches, (2) acquaintance who knows your reputation, (3) mutual connection who forwards, (4) cold contact with common ground, (5) cold contact with no connection

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Networking done 12+ months before graduation yields authentic conversations, reciprocal relationships, and discovery of unknown opportunities; networking done in desperation is detectable and limited to only what is currently available
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate months remaining before your target start date or OPT authorization deadline and compare against recommended lead times (9–12 months for internships, 12–18 months for international students)

decision_factors.why_it_matters: International students have a hard constraint (OPT deadline) that compresses all other timelines and must be proactively communicated to recruiters; failure to manage this creates last-minute scrambles with no recovery options
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Know the exact date of authorization expiration, whether sponsorship is required and when, and whether each target company has a history of sponsoring the relevant visa type

decision_factors.factor: Ability to articulate outcomes, not just experience
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Hiring managers consistently distinguish candidates who can quantify impact ('reduced latency by 40%') from those who describe activity ('worked on backend systems'); this is a filtering criterion, not a tiebreaker
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Test yourself: can you explain your most impactful project in 90 seconds in a way that makes a non-technical hiring manager want to know more?

decision_factors.factor: Existing network density at target companies
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Most offers are won at steps 2–4 of the funnel (network activation, outreach, conversation) not at step 5 (application); having zero contacts at a target company means starting from the least efficient entry point
decision_factors.how_to_assess: For each company on your target list, count how many current employees you have spoken to in the last 6 months

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The hidden job market is most accessible to people who are findable — through blog posts, open source, talks, LinkedIn articles; invisibility forecloses inbound opportunities entirely
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Search your own name and relevant skill keywords on LinkedIn and GitHub; assess whether a recruiter searching for your skill set would find you without knowing your name

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Generic interest (applied everywhere, prepared for nowhere) is one of the most common reasons candidates fail to get offers; companies detect and penalize it through interview questions about motivation
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Write one sentence explaining why you want to work at each specific company that could not apply to any other company

decision_factors.factor: Portfolio and proof of work completeness
decision_factors.why_it_matters: These must be completed before outreach begins; initiating networking without published proof of work wastes the relationship window because you cannot demonstrate capability when asked
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Is your portfolio publicly accessible and does it include work a recruiter or hiring manager could evaluate without asking you for context?

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Starting job search only after graduation or when urgency is felt
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The best opportunities — those accessed through warm relationships, early referrals, and insider knowledge of unlisted roles — are already foreclosed; you can only compete for what is currently posted and visible
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students on OPT deadlines, who have a compressing authorization window that leaves no slack for slow-cycle relationship building

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Spending most job search time on applications to posted jobs
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: ATS systems filter out 75–90% of applicants before human eyes see them, meaning the majority of this effort produces zero result; the activity feels productive while generating almost no pipeline advancement
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who optimize resumes extensively but do no outreach — they invest the most in the stage with the lowest uncontrolled conversion rate

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Targeting only brand-name companies you have heard of
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You compete in the most crowded applicant pools against the most well-networked candidates, while excellent companies with higher conversion rates and less competition go uncontacted
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who rely on brand recognition as a proxy for visa sponsorship likelihood without verifying actual sponsorship history at lesser-known but equally capable companies

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Asking for a referral or job in the first informational interview conversation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The social obligation that would have naturally developed over a genuine conversation is destroyed; the contact is less likely to help than a stranger because the transactional framing has poisoned the relationship
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High-urgency job seekers in desperation phase who cannot afford to invest in relationship development and attempt to shortcut the process

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Building your network only when you need a job
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Every outreach is immediately recognizable as transactional; you have no reciprocal value to offer because you have built no reputation as a connector; contacts sense the pressure and respond accordingly
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who treat networking as a job search tactic rather than a career-long practice, and who discover at graduation that their network consists only of classmates in the same situation

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Practicing interview responses silently and internally rather than out loud
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The skill being tested — coherent thinking under social pressure, with a real person in real time — is never actually practiced; performance in the actual interview is not predicted by how well the internal rehearsal went
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High-intelligence candidates who are comfortable with conceptual understanding but have not built the specific verbal fluency skill that interviews require

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating job search as a post-graduation problem rather than a concurrent multi-semester project
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The cost of inaction is invisible until the deadline arrives; students prioritize coursework with immediate feedback loops and defer job search because consequences feel distant
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time urgency is felt, the relationship-building window is closed; only posted, competitive, ATS-filtered roles remain available; international students with OPT deadlines face the highest cost
warning_signs: Having no conversations with current employees at target companies 6 months before graduation

failure_patterns.pattern: Optimizing the resume while neglecting the network
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Resume editing is a controllable, comfortable task with visible output; outreach involves rejection risk and social discomfort, so students substitute resume polish for pipeline activity
failure_patterns.consequence: Strong credentials attached to no relationships enter ATS systems and are filtered out before human review; effort produces no interviews
warning_signs: Spending hours tweaking resume formatting while sending fewer than 5 outreach messages per week
Describing your job search strategy in terms of resume versions rather than conversations had

failure_patterns.pattern: Generic interest across all applications with no company-specific preparation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Volume feels like strategy; sending 100 applications feels more productive than deep research on 10 companies; students assume differentiation happens at the resume level
failure_patterns.consequence: Fails the 'specific reason why this company' filter in interviews; produces no inside knowledge advantage; misses unlisted roles that require a relationship to discover
warning_signs: Unable to answer 'why us specifically' without mentioning brand name or size
No conversations with employees at any target company before applying

failure_patterns.pattern: Strong credentials but inability to explain work in plain language under pressure
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Technical training emphasizes depth and correctness; communication under time pressure with non-technical audiences is never practiced in academic settings; students assume capability implies communicability
failure_patterns.consequence: Fails to convert interviews into offers despite genuine competence; loses to candidates with slightly weaker credentials but stronger communication
warning_signs: Cannot explain most impactful project in 90 seconds without being prompted or corrected
Answers to 'tell me about yourself' consistently exceed 3 minutes

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating rejection as failure rather than as pipeline signal
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Rejection is emotionally costly; students reduce applications to reduce exposure to rejection rather than analyzing patterns and adjusting; the sunk cost of each application makes rejection feel personal
failure_patterns.consequence: No improvement across application cycles; same weaknesses persist; pipeline volume drops below the threshold needed to generate offers
warning_signs: Not tracking rejections or asking for feedback
Reducing application volume after rejection rather than analyzing and adjusting

diagnostic_questions.question: Can you explain your most impactful project in 90 seconds in a way that would make a non-technical hiring manager want to know more?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has translated technical work into outcome-oriented language that survives cross-functional communication — a direct hiring manager filter
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specific metric or outcome do you lead with, and how do you know it was impactful relative to alternatives?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is the barrier a lack of quantified outcomes in your work, or an inability to frame existing outcomes for a non-technical audience?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you spoken to at least one current employee at each of your target companies in the last 6 months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is operating at steps 2–4 of the funnel (relationship-based) or defaulting to step 5 (application-based); reveals the actual state of their network at each target
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What did you learn from those conversations that changed how you're positioning yourself or which roles you're targeting?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What is preventing outreach — absence of any connection point, fear of rejection, or belief that applications alone are sufficient?

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you know the exact timeline of your work authorization status and have you communicated it clearly to recruiters?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has internalized the OPT deadline as a hard constraint that compresses all other timelines, and whether they are managing recruiter expectations proactively
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you verified which of your target companies have a track record of sponsoring the specific visa type you will need?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: When is your OPT authorization deadline, and have you calculated backward from that date to determine when job search must have already begun?

diagnostic_questions.question: Can you give a specific reason — one that could not apply to any other company — for why you want to work at each company you are actively applying to?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has done genuine company research or is applying by brand recognition; predicts performance on 'why us' interview questions and whether outreach messages will read as authentic
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: How did you develop that specific reason — through employee conversations, product research, or public information?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you applying to these companies because of genuine fit signals or because of name recognition and perceived safety in volume?

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you practiced behavioral interview responses out loud with another person in the last two weeks?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has built the actual verbal fluency skill that interviews test, or whether they have only studied the framework — a distinction that predicts performance under real interview conditions
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What feedback did you receive, and what specific response weaknesses are you actively working on?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Is the barrier access to a practice partner, belief that internal rehearsal is equivalent, or underestimation of how different live performance is from studied preparation?

diagnostic_questions.question: How far in advance did you begin building your network at your target companies?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is in early-networking mode (authentic, reciprocal, time to discover unknown opportunities) or late-networking mode (high pressure, transactional, limited to currently available roles)
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What reciprocal value have you offered to contacts — introductions, relevant information, collaboration — that distinguishes you as a connector rather than just a job seeker?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Given the remaining time before your deadline, which relationship-building steps can still be completed meaningfully versus which require a different strategy?

industry_realities.common_belief: Sending many applications is a job search strategy
industry_realities.actual_reality: ATS systems filter out 75–90% of applicants before any human sees them; the application stage is a validation mechanism for opportunities already created through relationships, not a discovery mechanism
industry_realities.implication: Time allocated to volume applications beyond a threshold returns less than time allocated to relationship activation; the application should be the last step of a process that began with network contact

industry_realities.common_belief: The resume is the primary job search tool
industry_realities.actual_reality: The resume validates a candidate who is already known to a human reviewer; it does not discover opportunities; most offers are won at steps 2–4 of the funnel before the resume is ever seen
industry_realities.implication: Resume optimization should follow, not lead, network activation; a strong resume submitted cold converts at dramatically lower rates than the same resume submitted with a referral

industry_realities.common_belief: Asking someone for a referral is what networking is for
industry_realities.actual_reality: Referrals are an outcome of relationships, not the purpose of a conversation; asking for a referral before establishing genuine connection destroys the social obligation that would have created the referral naturally
industry_realities.implication: Informational interviews should be structured as knowledge-seeking conversations with no explicit ask; the referral request, if it comes, should emerge from a relationship the contact feels is genuine

industry_realities.common_belief: The job market consists of posted positions on job boards
industry_realities.actual_reality: The hidden job market — positions filled before posting — is most accessible through former managers, weak ties, public visibility, and tracking companies in active growth; most experienced hires bypass the public posting process entirely
industry_realities.implication: A job search strategy built only on job board monitoring will systematically miss the highest-quality opportunities filled through network channels before they become visible

industry_realities.common_belief: Strong credentials and technical ability are the primary differentiators at hiring
industry_realities.actual_reality: Hiring managers consistently report that what distinguishes hired candidates is demonstrated outcomes (not just experience), curiosity about the specific company, clear communication under pressure, and a narrative of fit — not raw credential strength
industry_realities.implication: Candidates should invest as much preparation in articulating impact and company-specific reasoning as in technical interview preparation; communication skill is a filtering criterion, not a tiebreaker

industry_realities.common_belief: Networking close to graduation is sufficient if done intensively
industry_realities.actual_reality: Late networking is categorically different from early networking; desperation is detectable, reciprocal relationships cannot be built in weeks, and you are limited to only what is currently available rather than discovering unknown opportunities
industry_realities.implication: The network available at graduation is the one built over the preceding two years; intensive late-stage networking can only partially compensate for the relational capital that early networking would have created

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats job search as a funnel entered at the application stage; optimizes resume as the primary lever; waits for responses after submitting
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats job search as a relationship activation problem; builds network before need arises; uses application as a validation step after human contact is established
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The entry point into the funnel determines which conversion rates apply; entering at step 5 means competing against 75–90% ATS filtration with no relationship advantage

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats each rejection as a failure signal; either reduces application volume to avoid further rejection or continues without changing approach
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats rejection as a filtering mechanism and pipeline data point; asks for feedback, tracks patterns across rejections, and adjusts approach based on aggregate signal
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Only candidates who analyze rejections improve across cycles; those who avoid or ignore rejection data repeat the same failure patterns until the authorization deadline passes

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Defaults to Type C (browsing, optimizing resume abstractly) and Type B (applying to posted jobs) because they are comfortable and produce visible output
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Prioritizes Type A (cold outreach, referral requests, networking with strangers) despite discomfort because return per hour is highest even when per-message response rate is low
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Activity type determines pipeline generation rate; comfort-seeking job search produces the feeling of productivity with a fraction of the actual results

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Targets companies by brand recognition and name familiarity; applies broadly to maximize perceived chances; prepares generically
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Targets companies by researched fit (culture, tech stack, team problems, recent news); maintains a focused list with specific rationale; prepares with inside knowledge from employee conversations
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Brand-name targeting concentrates effort in the most competitive applicant pools; researched targeting reveals less-competed opportunities and produces the company-specific reasoning that distinguishes hired candidates

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Studies STAR framework, reads about behavioral questions, rehearses answers mentally; believes understanding the framework equals readiness
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Drills responses out loud with live partners; treats interview preparation as skill-building requiring physical practice, not knowledge acquisition
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The skill being evaluated in interviews — coherent verbal thinking under social pressure — is not developed by reading or internal rehearsal; performance predicts only from practiced performance

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Initiates networking when job search urgency is felt; frames every interaction around job search needs; asks for referrals as the primary objective of conversations
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Builds network continuously before need arises; frames interactions around genuine knowledge exchange and relationship development; earns referrals as an outcome of established connection
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Transactional networking under time pressure is detectable and produces lower response rates; relationship-based networking produces the social obligation that converts to referrals and insider access

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of what hiring managers actually evaluate
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Assumes credentials, GPA, and technical depth are the primary differentiators; focuses preparation on proving technical ability
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Understands that demonstrated outcomes, company-specific curiosity, communication clarity under pressure, and narrative fit are the actual hiring filters; prepares accordingly across all dimensions
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Misdiagnnosing the evaluation criteria causes candidates to over-invest in one dimension (technical) while leaving the actual differentiating factors (communication, specificity, narrative) underdeveloped
